draft.introduction: In Nicomachean Ethics X.7, Aristotle argues that the contemplative life is the highest form of happiness because it exercises the divine element in us. Contemplation is the most self-sufficient, continuous, and pleasant of all activities.

translation.text: If happiness is activity in accordance with virtue, it is reasonable that it should be in accordance with the highest virtue, and this will be that of the best thing in us. Whether it is intellect or something else that seems to rule and lead by nature and to have knowledge of noble and divine things—the activity of this in accordance with its proper virtue will be complete happiness. And this activity is contemplation.
translation.original_text: εἰ δ᾽ ἐστὶν ἡ εὐδαιμονία κατ᾽ ἀρετὴν ἐνέργεια, εὔλογον κατὰ τὴν κρατίστην

translator_notes.note: Aristotle’s claim that contemplation (θεωρία) is the highest happiness because it exercises “the divine in us” connects ethics directly to theology. The Neoplatonists read this passage as Aristotle’s endorsement of the philosophical life as assimilation to God.
